name: Oxygen
name_origin: From Greek 'oxys' (sharp, acid) and 'genes' (forming), meaning 'acid-former'; named by Antoine Lavoisier in 1777, who incorrectly believed oxygen was essential to all acids.

discovery.discoverer: Carl Wilhelm Scheele (1771-72, unpublished) and Joseph Priestley (1774, independent); named by Antoine Lavoisier

occurrence.abundance_summary: Oxygen is the most abundant element in Earth's crust by mass (~46%), the third most abundant in the universe, and makes up ~21% of the atmosphere as O2. It is bound in water, silicates, oxides, carbonates, and nearly all biomolecules.
states: in carbonates, sulfates, and most biomolecules

compounds.name: Water
compounds.summary: The universal solvent, essential to all known life and the most abundant compound on Earth's surface.

compounds.name: Carbon dioxide
compounds.summary: A trace atmospheric gas central to photosynthesis, respiration, and anthropogenic climate change.

compounds.name: Silicon dioxide
compounds.summary: The primary component of sand, quartz, and glass; the most common mineral on Earth's surface.

compounds.name: Iron(III) oxide
compounds.summary: The chief component of common rust and the iron ore hematite.

compounds.name: Ozone
compounds.summary: A reactive triatomic allotrope that filters UV radiation in the stratosphere but is harmful as a ground-level pollutant.

compounds.name: Hydrogen peroxide
compounds.summary: A versatile oxidizer used as a disinfectant, hair bleach, and rocket propellant.

uses: Steelmaking via the basic oxygen process (the largest single industrial use)
Medical oxygen therapy for patients with hypoxia and respiratory disease
Liquid oxygen oxidizer for rocket propulsion (LOX)
Life support in submarines, spacecraft, and high-altitude aircraft
reactivity_summary: Oxygen is a powerful oxidizer that combines directly with most elements to form oxides; only the noble gases and a few unreactive metals resist it. O2 supports combustion and aerobic respiration, while the more reactive ozone (O3) is a strong UV absorber and disinfectant. Oxygen's high electronegativity (second only to fluorine) drives much of acid-base and redox chemistry.
isotopes_summary: Oxygen has three stable isotopes: 16O (~99.76%), 18O (~0.20%), and 17O (~0.04%). The 18O/16O ratio in ice cores, foraminifera, and water is a key proxy for paleotemperature reconstruction.

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Oxygen
CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
